rows.question: Which EU act sets maximum residue levels for pesticides such as acetamiprid and tebuconazole in or on certain products?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015R0401 - Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401 of 25 February 2015, which amends Annexes II and III to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005 on maximum residue levels of pesticides.
rows.actualResponse:
The EU act that directly sets maximum residue levels (MRLs) for pesticides such as **acetamiprid** and **tebuconazole** in or on certain products is **Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401**.

---

## 📋 Key Act: Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401

**COMMISSION REGULATION (EU) 2015/401** of 25 February 2015 amends Annexes II and III to **Regulation (EC) No 396/2005** of the European Parliament and of the Council, specifically as regards maximum residue levels for **acetamiprid**, chromafenozide, cyazofamid, dicamba, difenoconazole, fenpyrazamine, fluazinam, formetanate, nicotine, penconazole, pymetrozine, pyraclostrobin, tau-fluvalinate, and **tebuconazole** in or on certain products (CELEX ID: **32015R0401**).[1]

### Why Were the MRLs Modified?

- **Acetamiprid:** In accordance with Article 6(2) and (4) of Regulation (EC) No 396/2005, an application was submitted for acetamiprid on **apricots and tree nuts**. The applicant claimed that the authorised uses of that substance on such crops in the **United States** lead to residues exceeding the existing MRLs, and that higher MRLs are necessary to avoid trade barriers for the importation of those crops.[1]

- **Tebuconazole:** For tebuconazole, an application was made for **poppy seed**.[1]

---

## 📌 Parent Regulation: Regulation (EC) No 396/2005

The parent framework under which MRLs are established is **Regulation (EC) No 396/2005** of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 February 2005, which governs maximum residue levels of pesticides in or on food and feed of plant and animal origin. For **acetamiprid, cyazofamid, formetanate, pymetrozine, pyraclostrobin, and tebuconazole**, MRLs were originally set in **Annex II** to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005.[1]

---

## 📌 Related Act: Commission Regulation (EU) 2014/61 (CELEX: 32014R0061)

Another relevant regulation also reviews and revises MRLs for **tebuconazole** specifically:

For **tebuconazole**, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) submitted a reasoned opinion on the existing MRLs and **proposed to change the residue definition**. It recommended **lowering the MRLs** for apples, pears, apricots, peaches, table grapes, strawberries, blackberries, raspberries, blueberries, cranberries, currants, gooseberries, elderberries, carrots, parsnips, parsley roots, garlic, tomatoes, cucumber, pumpkins, watermelons, broccoli, cauliflower, head cabbage, asparagus, leek, soya bean, rye grain, wheat grain, and ruminant milk, while for other products it recommended raising or keeping the existing MRLs.[2]

---

|---|---|---|
| **32015R0401** | Commission Regulation (EU) 2015/401 | Directly amends MRLs for **acetamiprid** and **tebuconazole** in or on certain products by modifying Annexes II and III to Regulation (EC) No 396/2005. |
| **32014R0061** | Commission Regulation (EU) 2014/61 | Reviews and revises existing MRLs specifically for **tebuconazole** across a wide range of food products, based on EFSA's reasoned opinion. |

rows.question: Which EU act includes pyriproxyfen as an active substance for biocidal products, and which Member State acted as Rapporteur for its evaluation?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32013L0005 - Commission Directive 2013/5/EU of 14 February 2013, which adds pyriproxyfen to Annex I of Directive 98/8/EC. The Netherlands was the designated Rapporteur Member State.
rows.actualResponse:
Great news — the answer is found directly in the retrieved document. Here is a full summary:

---

## 🇪🇺 EU Act Including Pyriproxyfen as an Active Substance for Biocidal Products

### The Act

The relevant EU act is **Commission Directive 2013/5/EU** of 14 February 2013, which amends Directive 98/8/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council **to include pyriproxyfen as an active substance in Annex I** thereto *(Text with EEA relevance)*.[1]

---

### Background & Scope

Pyriproxyfen was part of a list of active substances to be assessed, with a view to their possible inclusion in Annex I, IA or IB to Directive 98/8/EC, as established by Commission Regulation (EC) No 1451/2007. It was specifically evaluated for use in **product-type 18: insecticides, acaricides and products to control other arthropods**, as defined in Annex V to that Directive.[1]

---

### Rapporteur Member State

**The Netherlands** were designated as the **Rapporteur Member State** and submitted the competent authority report, together with a recommendation, to the Commission on **2 October 2009**, in accordance with Article 10(5) and (7) of Regulation (EC) No 1451/2007.[1]

---

### Key Findings & Conditions

The evaluations showed that biocidal products used as insecticides, acaricides and products to control other arthropods containing pyriproxyfen may be expected to satisfy the requirements laid down in Article 5 of Directive 98/8/EC. It was therefore considered appropriate to include pyriproxyfen for use in **product-type 18** in Annex I to that Directive.[1]Member States are required to ensure that products authorised for professionals are used with appropriate personal protective equipment, and that products are not authorised for direct use on surface water unless risks to aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems and groundwater can be reduced to an acceptable level.[2]

---

rows.question: Which EU regulation amended the rules on the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32012R1028 - Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 October 2012, amending Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 as regards the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers.
rows.actualResponse:
The EU regulation that amended the rules on the **single payment scheme and support to vine-growers** is:

---

## 📜 Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012 — CELEX ID: **32012R1028**

**REGULATION (EU) No 1028/2012 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of **25 October 2012**, amending Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 as regards the **regime of the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers**.[1]

---

### 🔍 Background & Purpose

Article 103o of Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 (the Single CMO Regulation) provided for a possibility for Member States to grant **decoupled aid under the single payment scheme to vine-growers**, and several Member States had used this specific support measure.[1]However, the fact that Member States could modify transfers to the single payment scheme from support programmes once a year — while support programmes had a five-year duration and payment entitlements were granted for an indeterminate period — resulted in **administrative and budgetary burdens**.[1]

---

### 🎯 Key Changes Introduced

1. **Simplification**: In order to simplify the management of this specific support measure and to ensure its consistency with the objectives of the rules for direct support schemes for farmers, it was deemed appropriate to **convert it into the possibility for Member States to definitively decrease the funds allocated to wine sector support programmes** and thereby increase the national ceilings for direct payments.[1]

2. **Member State Option (by 1 August 2013)**: Member States were given the option to decide to reduce, from 2015, the amount available for support programmes in order to increase their national ceilings for direct payments. The reduced amount would **definitively remain in the national ceilings** and no longer be available for other measures in the wine support programmes.[1]

3. **Support for 2014**: Member States were allowed to continue applying the support under Article 103o for **2014** by allocating payment entitlements to vine-growers (with a decision deadline of 1 December 2012).[1]

4. For 2014, the support was to **remain in the single payment scheme and no longer be available** under Article 103k(3) for the wine sector measures, and it would proportionately reduce the funds available for those measures in the support programmes.[2]

---

|---|---|---|
| **32012R1028** | Regulation (EU) No 1028/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council | Directly amends Council Regulation (EC) No 1234/2007 on the single payment scheme and support to vine-growers |

rows.question: Which EU regulation lays down the model for operational programmes under the Investment for growth and jobs goal for the European Structural and Investment Funds?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32014R0288 - Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 288/2014 of 25 February 2014, adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 1303/2013 (and Regulation (EU) No 1299/2013 for cooperation programmes).
rows.actualResponse:
The EU regulation you are looking for is **Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 288/2014** (CELEX ID: **32014R0288**).

---

## 📋 Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 288/2014

**COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) No 288/2014** of **25 February 2014** lays down rules pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 1303/2013 of the European Parliament and of the Council — which establishes common provisions on the European Regional Development Fund, the European Social Fund, the Cohesion Fund, the European Agricultural Fund for Rural Development, and the European Maritime and Fisheries Fund — **with regard to the model for operational programmes under the Investment for growth and jobs goal**. It also lays down rules pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 1299/2013 with regard to the model for cooperation programmes under the European territorial cooperation goal.[1]

---

### 🔑 Key Provisions

- **Article 1(1)** of the Regulation explicitly states that the model for preparing **operational programmes under the Investment for growth and jobs goal** is set out in **Annex I** to the Regulation, while the model for cooperation programmes under the European territorial cooperation goal is set out in **Annex II**.[2]

- The models will form the basis for the development of the **electronic data exchange system** referred to in Article 74(4) of Regulation (EU) No 1303/2013, as regards the content and submission of operational programmes and cooperation programmes. They establish the manner in which data on operational programmes and cooperation programmes will be entered into the system for electronic data exchange.[1]

- The model for operational programmes corresponds to the structure of the operational programme set out in **Article 96 of Regulation (EU) 1303/2013**, and is intended to ensure that information is **consistent, comparable, and, where necessary, can be aggregated**. In addition to structured data, the models provide for the option to submit unstructured information in the form of compulsory or non-compulsory annexes.[2]

- The model also **identifies elements which may be presented in the Partnership Agreement only**, in accordance with Article 96(8) of Regulation (EU) No 1303/2013, and specifies sections that **should not be completed by Member States** if operational programmes are dedicated exclusively to technical assistance or to the Youth Employment Initiative.[2]

- The Regulation **entered into force on the day following its publication** in the Official Journal of the European Union, and is **binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States**.[2]

---

rows.question: Which EU regulation authorised the use of methacrylate copolymers as food additives in solid food supplements?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32013R0816 - Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013 of 28 August 2013, amending Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 on food additives.
rows.actualResponse:
The relevant EU regulation is **Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013** (CELEX ID: **32013R0816**). Here is a detailed breakdown:

---

## 📋 Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013

**Full Title:** *Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013 of 28 August 2013 amending Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008 as regards the use of Neutral methacrylate copolymer and Anionic methacrylate copolymer in solid food supplements.*

---

### 🔑 Key Points

- Applications for authorisation of the use of **Anionic methacrylate copolymer** and **Neutral methacrylate copolymer** as glazing agents in solid food supplements were submitted on 25 and 27 April 2009 and were made available to the Member States.[1]

- The **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)** evaluated the safety of both substances when used as food additives and concluded that their use in solid food supplements at the proposed use levels **is not of a safety concern**.[1]

- There is a **technological need** for their use:
  - **Neutral methacrylate copolymer** is intended to be used as a **sustained-release glazing agent**, allowing the continuous dissolution of a nutrient over a defined time.
  - **Anionic methacrylate copolymer** is intended to be used as a glazing agent to **protect the stomach against irritating ingredients** and/or to protect sensitive nutrients against disintegration by gastric acid.[1]

- As a result of the authorisation, **E 1206** was assigned as the E-number to Neutral methacrylate copolymer and **E 1207** was assigned to Anionic methacrylate copolymer.[1]

- Additionally, specifications were also adopted for **Neutral methacrylate copolymer (E 1206)** and **Anionic methacrylate copolymer (E 1207)**, with purity criteria for arsenic, lead, mercury, and cadmium following the same approach as those for Basic methacrylate copolymer (E 1205).[2]

### 📌 Legal Basis

- The regulation amends **Annex II to Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008** of the European Parliament and of the Council on food additives, and the Annex to **Commission Regulation (EU) No 231/2012** as regards the specifications for Basic methacrylate copolymer (E 1205), Neutral methacrylate copolymer, and Anionic methacrylate copolymer.[1]

- It also notes that Commission Regulation (EU) No 1129/2011 had previously authorised the use of **Basic methacrylate copolymer (E 1205)** in solid food supplements.[2]

---

### 📚 Sources

| CELEX ID | Title | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **32013R0816** | Commission Regulation (EU) No 816/2013 | Directly authorises the use of Neutral methacrylate copolymer (E 1206) and Anionic methacrylate copolymer (E 1207) as food additives (glazing agents) in solid food supplements. |

rows.question:
What does the 2015 EU Decision concerning restrictive measures in view of the situation in Libya change?

Metadata filters (apply with AND):
- Year: 2015
- Document type: Decision
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015D0382 - Council Decision (CFSP) 2015/382 of 6 March 2015 amends Decision 2011/137/CFSP, extending the criteria for the travel ban and asset freeze measures (implementing UN Security Council Resolution 2174 (2014)).
rows.actualResponse:
Here is a detailed summary of what **Council Decision (CFSP) 2015/382** changes regarding restrictive measures in view of the situation in Libya:

---

## 📋 Council Decision (CFSP) 2015/382 — Restrictive Measures Concerning Libya

**CELEX ID:** 32015D0382
**Date:** 6 March 2015
**Type:** Decision (CFSP) — *amending Decision 2011/137/CFSP*

---

### 🔍 Background & Context

On 28 February 2011, the Council originally adopted Decision 2011/137/CFSP. On 20 October 2014, the Council expressed its concern over the situation in Libya and stated it stood ready to implement UN Security Council Resolution (UNSCR) 2174 (2014) of 27 August 2014, in order to address threats to Libya's peace and stability. The Council concluded that those responsible for violence, and those who obstruct or undermine Libya's democracy, must be held accountable.[1]UNSCR 2174 (2014) extends the application of the travel ban and asset freeze measures — as set out in paragraph 22 of UNSCR 1970 (2011) and paragraph 23 of UNSCR 1973 (2011) — to include persons and entities that provide support for acts that threaten the peace, stability or security of Libya, or obstruct or undermine the successful completion of its political transition.[1]

---

### ✏️ Key Changes Introduced

#### 1. Expansion of the Travel Ban (Article 5)
Decision 2011/137/CFSP is amended to add a new point **(c)** to Article 5(1), extending the travel ban to cover persons **not covered by Annex I** to that Decision who are engaged in or providing support for acts that threaten the peace, stability or security of Libya, or obstruct or undermine the successful completion of its political transition, including by:
- **(i)** Planning, directing, or committing acts that violate international human rights law or international humanitarian law, or acts that constitute human rights abuses, in Libya;
- **(ii)** Attacks against any air, land, or sea port in Libya, or against a Libyan State institution or installation, or against any foreign mission in Libya;
- **(iii)** Providing support for armed groups or criminal networks through the illicit exploitation of crude oil or any other natural resources in Libya;
- **(iv)** Acting for or on behalf of, or at the direction of, listed persons or entities (as listed in Annex II to the Decision).[1]

#### 2. Expansion of the Asset Freeze (Article 6)
Similarly, a new point **(c)** is added to Article 6(1) — the asset freeze provision — extending it to persons and entities **not covered by Annex III** to the Decision, using the same categories of conduct described above (threats to peace/stability, attacks on infrastructure, support for armed groups through illicit exploitation of natural resources, or acting on behalf of listed persons/entities as listed in **Annex IV** to the Decision).[2]

#### 3. Amendments to Annexes (Listings)
Following the judgment of the General Court of 24 September 2014 in Case T-348/13 (*Kadhaf Al Dam v Council*), the entry concerning **Ahmed Mohammed Qadhaf Al-Dam** is deleted from **Annexes II and IV** to Decision 2011/137/CFSP. In addition, the entry concerning one other person is deleted from **Annex II**, and the entry concerning yet another person in **Annexes II and IV** is updated.[1]The amendments to Annexes II and IV are set out in the Annex to the 2015 Decision itself.[2]

#### 4. Entry into Force
This Decision entered into force on the day following that of its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union (adopted at Brussels, 6 March 2015).[2]

---

|---|---|---|

rows.question:
Which 2013 Directive in the Trade domain postpones the transposition and application dates of the Solvency II Directive?

Metadata filters (apply with AND):
- Year: 2013
- Document type: Directive
- Policy domain: Trade
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32013L0058 - Directive 2013/58/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 December 2013, amending Directive 2009/138/EC (Solvency II) as regards the dates of transposition, application and the repeal of Solvency I directives.
rows.actualResponse:
The document you are looking for is **Directive 2013/58/EU** (CELEX: **32013L0058**). Here is a detailed breakdown:

---

## 📄 Directive 2013/58/EU — Postponement of Solvency II Dates

**CELEX ID:** 32013L0058
**Title:** *Directive 2013/58/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 December 2013 amending Directive 2009/138/EC (Solvency II) as regards the date for its transposition and the date of its application, and the date of repeal of certain Directives (Solvency I)*

---

### 🔍 Background & Purpose

Directive 2009/138/EC (Solvency II) provides a modern, risk-based system for the regulation and supervision of insurance and reinsurance undertakings of the Union, essential to ensure a safe and solid insurance sector.[1]The Omnibus II proposal, adopted by the Commission on 19 January 2011, included provisions to postpone the date for transposition and the date of application of Directive 2009/138/EC, and the date of repeal of Solvency I, and also served as a means to adapt Directive 2009/138/EC to the entry into force of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union.[1]Given the complexity of the Omnibus II proposal, there was a risk that it would not have entered into force before the date for transposition and the date of application of Directive 2009/138/EC. Leaving those dates unchanged would result in Directive 2009/138/EC being implemented before the entry into force of the transitional rules and of relevant adaptations provided for by the Omnibus II proposal.[1]

---

### 📅 Specific Date Changes Made

The following amendments were made to Directive 2009/138/EC:
- In **Article 309(1)**, the transposition date of **'30 June 2013'** was replaced by **'31 March 2015'**, and the application date of **'1 January 2014'** was replaced by **'1 January 2016'**.
- In **Article 310**, the date of **'1 January 2014'** was replaced by **'1 January 2016'**.
- In **Article 311**, the date of **'1 January 2014'** was replaced by **'1 January 2016'**.[2]

---

### ⚖️ Legal Rationale

In order to avoid overly burdensome legislative obligations for Member States, it was considered appropriate to postpone the date for transposition and the date of application of Directive 2009/138/EC, allowing supervisors and insurance and reinsurance undertakings sufficient time to prepare for the application of the new supervisory architecture.[2]It was also noted that the postponed dates for transposition and application of Directive 2009/138/EC should also apply to the amendments thereto made by Directive 2011/89/EC, and that for reasons of legal certainty, the date of repeal of Solvency I should be postponed accordingly.[2]Given the very short period of time before the relevant dates laid down in Directive 2009/138/EC, this Directive was required to enter into force without delay.[2]

---

rows.question:
Which 2015 Transport Directive updates the EU rules on port reception facilities for ship-generated waste to reflect the revised MARPOL Annex V garbage categories?

Metadata filters (apply with AND):
- Year: 2015
- Document type: Directive
- Policy domain: Transport
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015L2087 - Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087 of 18 November 2015, amending Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC on port reception facilities for ship-generated waste and cargo residues.
rows.actualResponse:
## 🚢 2015 Transport Directive on Port Reception Facilities & MARPOL Annex V

The directive you are looking for is **Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087**.

---

### 📄 Document Overview

**CELEX ID:** `32015L2087`
**Title:** Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087 of 18 November 2015 amending Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council on port reception facilities for ship-generated waste and cargo residues *(Text with EEA relevance)*
**Policy Domain:** Transport | **Document Type:** Directive | **Year:** 2015[1]

---

### 🔍 Why Was This Directive Adopted?

On 15 July 2011, the International Maritime Organisation (IMO) adopted **Resolution MEPC.201(62)**, amending Annex V to the MARPOL Convention on the prevention of pollution by garbage from ships, by introducing a **new and more detailed categorization of garbage**. The amended Annex V to the MARPOL Convention entered into force on **1 January 2013**.[1]This new categorization was reflected in:
- **IMO Circular MEPC.1/Circ.644/Rev.1** — providing a standard format for the **advance notification form** for waste delivery to port reception facilities.
- **IMO Circular MEPC.1/Circ.645/Rev.1** — providing a standard format for the **waste delivery receipt** following a ship's use of port reception facilities.[1]

---

### ⚖️ What Does the Directive Change?

In the interest of **consistency with IMO measures** and to avoid uncertainty amongst port users and authorities, **Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC** — which contains the different types and amounts of waste and cargo residues to be delivered or retained on board — was adapted to the **new categorisation of garbage** of the amended MARPOL Annex V.[1]

Additionally, to improve the regime established under Directive 2000/59/EC (which aims at reducing discharges of ship-generated waste into the sea), **information on the types and quantities of ship-generated waste actually delivered to port reception facilities** in the last port of delivery was required to be included in the Annex II table.[1]The exact data on types and amounts of ship-generated waste delivered is essential for an **accurate calculation of sufficient dedicated storage capacity** on a ship — a prerequisite for allowing the ship to proceed to the next port without having delivered its waste, and for a **proper selection of vessels for inspection**, contributing to more effective maritime traffic and reduced turnaround time in ports.[1]

---

### 📊 Data Collection & Monitoring

A systematic collection of accurate waste delivery data would allow **better statistical analysis of waste flow patterns in ports** and facilitate the establishment of an information and monitoring system. The monitoring and exchange of such information — including electronic waste notifications — is supported by the **Union maritime information and exchange system (SafeSeaNet)**, which should be linked to a reporting module within the **Port State Control database**.[2]

---

### 🗓️ Key Legal Provisions


- **Article 1:** Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC is replaced by the Annex to this Directive.
- **Article 2:** Member States were required to adopt and publish the necessary national laws and regulations by **9 December 2016** at the latest.
- **Article 3:** This Directive entered into force on the **twentieth day** following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union.
[2]

---

rows.question:
Which 2014 EU Regulation lays down the rules on the information that Member States must send under the European Maritime and Fisheries Fund?

Metadata filters (apply with AND):
- Year: 2014
- Document type: Regulation
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32014R1243 - Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1243/2014 of 20 November 2014, adopted pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 508/2014 on the European Maritime and Fisheries Fund.
rows.actualResponse:
The document you are looking for is **Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1243/2014**. Here are the details:

---

## 📄 CELEX 32014R1243 — Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1243/2014

**Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1243/2014** of **20 November 2014** lays down rules pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 508/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council on the **European Maritime and Fisheries Fund (EMFF)** with regard to the **information to be sent by Member States**, as well as on **data needs and synergies between potential data sources**.[1]

---

### 🎯 Subject Matter

**Article 1** establishes that this Regulation sets out the rules on the information to be sent to the Commission by the Member States in order to **permit the monitoring and evaluation of the operations** financed under the EMFF under shared management.[1]

---

### 📋 Key Obligations on Member States

Each Member State shall **record in its database** as referred to in Article 125(2) of Regulation (EU) No 1303/2013 and **provide to the Commission a list of data** containing the information referred to in Article 107(3) of Regulation (EU) No 508/2014, complying with the structure set out in **Annex I** to this Regulation.[1]The list of data shall be recorded and provided to the Commission for **each operation selected for funding** under the operational programme supported by the EMFF.[1]

### **COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) No 1259/2014**

---

### 📌 Subject Matter

This is the **Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1259/2014** of 24 November 2014, adopted **on the reimbursement, in accordance with Article 26(5) of Regulation (EU) No 1306/2013 of the European Parliament and of the Council, of the appropriations carried over from financial year 2014**.[1]

---

### 📏 The Carry-Over Limit

Such carry-over is **limited to 2% of the initial appropriations** and to the amount of the adjustment of direct payments as referred to in Article 11 of Council Regulation (EC) No 73/2009 which was applied during the preceding financial year. In accordance with Article 26(5) of Regulation (EU) No 1306/2013, by way of derogation, **Member States are to reimburse that carry-over to the final recipients** who are subject, in the financial year to which the appropriations are carried over, to the adjustment rate. **CELEX ID: 32014R1307**

---

### 🌿 Purpose & Subject Matter

**Commission Regulation (EU) No 1307/2014** of 8 December 2014 defines the criteria and geographic ranges of highly biodiverse grassland for the purposes of **Article 7b(3)(c) of Directive 98/70/EC** and **Article 17(3)(c) of Directive 2009/28/EC**.[1]

---

### ⚖️ The Two Directives It Serves

The Regulation simultaneously serves the biofuel sustainability provisions of **two Directives**:

1. **Directive 98/70/EC** — *relating to the quality of petrol and diesel fuels*
   Directive 98/70/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 October 1998 relates to the quality of petrol and diesel fuels and amending Council Directive 93/12/EEC, as amended by Directive 2009/30/EC. The relevant provision is the second subparagraph of **Article 7b(3)(c)**.[1]

2. **Directive 2009/28/EC** — *on the promotion of the use of energy from renewable sources*
   Directive 2009/28/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 23 April 2009 on the promotion of the use of energy from renewable sources and amending and subsequently repealing Directives 2001/77/EC and 2003/30/EC. The relevant provision is the second subparagraph of **Article 17(3)(c)**.[1]

### 🔗 Why These Two Directives?

Both Directives lay down that biofuels and bioliquids may only be counted towards the established targets and economic operators may only benefit from public support if they comply with the sustainability criteria laid down in those Directives. As part of this scheme, biofuels and bioliquids can only be counted towards the targets or benefit from public support in case they are **not made from raw material obtained from land that in or after January 2008 was highly biodiverse grassland**, unless in the case of non-natural highly biodiverse grasslands evidence is provided that the harvesting of the raw material is necessary to preserve its grassland status.[1]Article 17(3)(c) last subparagraph of Directive 2009/28/EC and Article 7b(3)(c) last subparagraph of Directive 98/70/EC specifically request the Commission to establish the criteria and geographic ranges to determine which grassland qualifies as highly biodiverse grassland.[1]

For the purposes of the EU, grasslands in the following geographic ranges shall **always** be regarded as highly biodiverse grassland:
1. Habitats listed in **Annex I to Council Directive 92/43/EEC**;
2. Habitats of significant importance for animal and plant species of Union interest listed in **Annexes II and IV to Directive 92/43/EEC**;
3. Habitats of significant importance for wild bird species listed in **Annex I to Directive 2009/147/EC**.[2]Highly biodiverse grassland in the European Union is not limited to those geographic ranges. Other grassland might fulfil the criteria for highly biodiverse grassland set out in Article 1.[2]

rows.actualResponse:
## Directive (EU) 2015/720 — Reducing Lightweight Plastic Carrier Bag Consumption

The directive you are looking for is **Directive (EU) 2015/720** (CELEX: **32015L0720**).

---

### Overview

**DIRECTIVE (EU) 2015/720 OF THE EUROPEAN PARLIAMENT AND OF THE COUNCIL** of **29 April 2015** amends **Directive 94/62/EC** as regards reducing the consumption of lightweight plastic carrier bags. It falls under the **Trade** policy domain and is classified as a **Directive** from **2015**.[1]Directive 94/62/EC was originally adopted to prevent or reduce the impact of packaging and packaging waste on the environment. Although plastic carrier bags constitute packaging within the meaning of that Directive, it did not contain specific measures on the consumption of such bags.[1]

---

### What Are "Lightweight Plastic Carrier Bags"?

**Lightweight plastic carrier bags** are plastic carrier bags with a **wall thickness below 50 microns**, which represent the vast majority of the total number of plastic carrier bags consumed in the Union. They are less frequently reused than thicker plastic carrier bags, become waste more quickly, and are more prone to littering due to their light weight.[1]

---

### Per-Person Consumption Targets

Member States must adopt measures ensuring that the **annual consumption level does not exceed**:
- **90 lightweight plastic carrier bags per person by 31 December 2019**, and
- **40 lightweight plastic carrier bags per person by 31 December 2025**,

or **equivalent targets set in weight**. Very lightweight plastic carrier bags may be excluded from national consumption objectives.[2]

---

### Additional Key Obligations

Member States must also adopt instruments ensuring that, **by 31 December 2018, lightweight plastic carrier bags are not provided free of charge** at the point of sale of goods or products, unless equally effective instruments are implemented. Very lightweight plastic carrier bags may be excluded from those measures.[2]Measures to be taken by Member States may involve the use of **economic instruments** such as pricing, taxes and levies — which have proved particularly effective in reducing consumption — and **marketing restrictions** such as bans, provided that these restrictions are proportionate and non-discriminatory.[3]From **27 May 2018**, Member States shall report on the **annual consumption** of lightweight plastic carrier bags when providing data on packaging and packaging waste to the Commission.[2]

---

## 📄 Commission Directive 2013/9/EU

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| **CELEX ID** | 32013L0009 |
| **Full Title** | Commission Directive 2013/9/EU of 11 March 2013 |
| **Policy Domain** | Law |
| **Document Type** | Directive |
| **Year** | 2013 |

---

### 🔧 What Does It Amend?

This is a Commission Directive of **11 March 2013** that amends **Annex III to Directive 2008/57/EC** of the European Parliament and of the Council on the interoperability of the rail system within the Community.[1]

---

### ♿ What Concern Does It Add?

The central new concern introduced is **accessibility** for persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility. Specifically:

1. **General Requirement (Section 1.6):** The "infrastructure" and "rolling stock" subsystems must be accessible to persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility in order to ensure access on an equal basis with others by way of the prevention or removal of barriers, and by way of other appropriate measures. This shall include the design, construction, renewal, upgrade, maintenance and operation of the relevant parts of the subsystems to which the public has access.[2]

2. **Operations & Telematics (Section 1.6.2):** The "operations" and "telematics applications for passengers" subsystems must provide for the necessary functionality required to facilitate access to persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility on an equal basis with others by way of the prevention or removal of barriers, and by way of other appropriate measures.[2]

3. **Infrastructure Subsystems (Section 2.1.2):** Infrastructure subsystems to which the public has access must be accessible to persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility.[2]

4. **Rolling Stock (Section 2.4.5):** Rolling stock subsystems to which the public has access must be accessible to persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility.[2]

5. **Operations (Section 2.6.4):** Appropriate steps must be taken to ensure that operating rules provide for the necessary functionality required to ensure accessibility for persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility.[2]

6. **Telematics Applications for Passengers (Section 2.7.5):** Appropriate steps must be taken to ensure that telematics applications for passengers subsystems provide for the necessary functionality required to ensure accessibility for persons with disabilities and persons with reduced mobility.[2]

---

### 🌍 Why Was This Change Made?

The United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, to which the European Union is a party, establishes accessibility as one of its general principles and requires that States Parties take appropriate measures to ensure to persons with disabilities access on an equal basis with others, including the identification and elimination of obstacles and barriers to accessibility in transportation. An adaptation of Annex III to Directive 2008/57/EC was therefore necessary in order to include therein an explicit reference to accessibility.[1]

---

### ⏱️ Transposition Deadline

Member States were required to bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive by **1 January 2014** at the latest.[2]

rows.question: Which EU directive amends the Council Directive relating to honey, notably clarifying the status of pollen?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32014L0063 - Directive 2014/63/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 May 2014, amending Council Directive 2001/110/EC relating to honey.
rows.actualResponse:
The directive you are looking for is **Directive 2014/63/EU** (CELEX: **32014L0063**). Here is a detailed summary:

---

## Directive 2014/63/EU — Amending Council Directive 2001/110/EC on Honey

### Overview

**Directive 2014/63/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council**, adopted on **15 May 2014**, amends **Council Directive 2001/110/EC** relating to honey.[1]

---

### Key Issue: The Status of Pollen

Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011 defines **'ingredient'** as any substance used in the manufacture or preparation of a food and still present in the finished product, even in altered form — a definition that implies a **deliberate use** of a substance. Taking into account the natural character of honey, and in particular the natural origin of the presence of constituents particular to honey, **pollen, being a natural constituent particular to honey, should not be considered to be an 'ingredient' of honey** within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011.[1]Available evidence, including empiric and scientific data, confirms that **bees are the origin of the presence of pollen in honey**. Pollen grains fall into nectar which is collected by bees, and in the hive, collected nectar containing pollen grains is transformed into honey. Pollen can therefore be said to enter the hive as a result of the activity of bees and is **naturally present in honey** regardless of whether or not food business operators extract that honey. Furthermore, the deliberate addition of pollen to honey by food business operators is **prohibited** under Directive 2001/110/EC.[1]

---

### Key Amendment Introduced

The directive adds the following provision to Article 2 of Directive 2001/110/EC:
> *"Pollen, being a natural constituent particular to honey, shall **not** be considered to be an ingredient, within the meaning of point (f) of Article 2(2) of Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011 of the European Parliament and of the Council, of the products defined in Annex I to this Directive."*[2]

---

### GMO Pollen Caveat

This Directive is **without prejudice** to the application of Regulation (EC) No 1829/2003 to honey containing **genetically modified pollen**, since such honey constitutes food produced from genetically modified organisms. Laying down a provision to the effect that pollen is not an ingredient of honey does **not** affect the Court's conclusion in Case C-442/09 that honey containing genetically modified pollen is subject to Regulation (EC) No 1829/2003, in particular to the requirements thereof concerning **authorisation prior to placing on the market, supervision and, where applicable, labelling**.[3]

---

### Other Amendments

The directive also clarifies the **labelling requirements** for cases where honey originates in more than one Member State or third country (replacing references to 'EC' with 'EU', e.g., "blend of EU honeys"), and reviews the scope of the power conferred on the Commission.[2]

---

The EU directive you are referring to is **Directive (EU) 2015/2203 of the European Parliament and of the Council** — **CELEX ID: 32015L2203**.

---

## 📄 Overview

**Directive (EU) 2015/2203** was adopted on **25 November 2015** and concerns the **approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to caseins and caseinates intended for human consumption**, and it explicitly **repeals Council Directive 83/417/EEC**.[1]

---

## 🔍 Why was it adopted?

Council Directive 83/417/EEC provided for the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to certain lactoproteins (caseins and caseinates) intended for human consumption. Since its entry into force, several changes had taken place, in particular the development of a comprehensive legal framework in the area of food law and the adoption of an international standard for edible casein products by the Codex Alimentarius Commission, which needed to be taken into account.[1]Furthermore, as a consequence of the entry into force of the Lisbon Treaty, the powers conferred on the Commission under Directive 83/417/EEC needed to be aligned to **Article 290 TFEU**. For the sake of clarity, Directive 83/417/EEC was therefore repealed and replaced with a new Directive.[1]

---

## 🥛 Key Definitions

For the purposes of this Directive:
- **'Edible acid casein'** means a milk product obtained by separating, washing and drying the acid-precipitated coagulum of skimmed milk and/or of other products obtained from milk.
- **'Edible rennet casein'** means a milk product obtained by separating, washing and drying the coagulum of skimmed milk and/or of other products obtained from milk, where the coagulum is obtained through the reaction of rennet or other coagulating enzymes.
- **'Edible caseinate'** means a milk product obtained by action of edible casein or edible casein curd coagulum with neutralizing agents, followed by drying.[2]

---

## 🎯 Objective

The objective of this Directive is to **facilitate, through approximation of the laws of the Member States, the free movement of caseins and caseinates intended for human consumption** while providing a high level of protection of health, and to bring existing provisions into line with general Union legislation on food and with international standards.[2]

---

## 🗑️ Repeal of the 1983 Directive

**Directive 83/417/EEC is repealed with effect from 22 December 2016.** References to the repealed Directive shall be construed as references to Directive (EU) 2015/2203, read in accordance with the correlation table in Annex III.[3]

## 📄 Regulation (EU) 2015/479 — Common Rules for Exports (Codification)

**CELEX ID:** 32015R0479
**Title:** Regulation (EU) 2015/479 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 March 2015 on common rules for exports (codification)
**Policy Domain:** Trade | **Document Type:** Regulation | **Year:** 2015[1]

---

### 🔍 Background & Purpose

Council Regulation (EC) No 1061/2009 had been substantially amended, and in the interests of **clarity and rationality**, that Regulation was codified into this new instrument. The common commercial policy is based on **uniform principles**, and common rules were laid down for exports from the Union.[1]

---

### 📌 Basic Principle

The foundational rule established by this Regulation is set out in **Article 1 (Chapter I — Basic Principle)**:

> *"The exportation of products from the Union to third countries shall be free, that is to say, they shall not be subject to any quantitative restriction, with the exception of those restrictions which are applied in conformity with this Regulation."*

In other words, **exports are almost completely liberalised** across all Member States, and it is accepted as a Union principle that exports to third countries are **not subject to any quantitative restriction**, subject to the exceptions provided for in the Regulation.[1]

---

### 🔎 Key Mechanisms

The Regulation establishes a **Union information and consultation procedure** (Chapter II). Member States may notify the Commission if unusual market developments suggest that protective measures might be necessary. Examination then takes place at Union level — covering export terms, conditions, trends, and the economic/commercial situation — to determine whether **Union surveillance** over certain exports or **interim protective measures** should be introduced as a safeguard against unforeseen practices.[1]Any **protective measures** necessitated by the interests of the Union must be adopted with due regard for existing **international obligations**. The Regulation applies to **all products**, whether industrial or agricultural, and its operation is complementary to the instruments establishing the common organisation of agricultural markets.[1]

### 🎯 Purpose & Background

Council Directive 2011/96/EU exempts dividends and other profit distributions paid by subsidiary companies to their parent companies from withholding taxes and eliminates double taxation of such income at the level of the parent company. However, it was deemed necessary to ensure that Directive 2011/96/EU is not abused by taxpayers who fall within the scope of its application.[1]Some Member States apply domestic or agreement-based provisions aimed at tackling tax evasion, tax fraud or abusive practices in a general or in a specific way. ### ⚖️ The Anti-Abuse Rule

The application of anti-abuse rules is required to be **proportionate** and serves the specific purpose of tackling an arrangement or a series of arrangements which are not genuine — that is, which do not reflect economic reality. ### ✈️ The "Stop the Clock" Mechanism

In order to sustain the momentum reached at the 38th Session of the ICAO Assembly in 2013 and facilitate progress at the upcoming 39th Session in 2016, the Regulation temporarily considers the requirements set out in Directive 2003/87/EC to be satisfied for the period until **31 December 2016** in respect of **flights to and from aerodromes in countries outside the European Economic Area (EEA).**[1]

Specifically, Article 28a of Directive 2003/87/EC (as inserted by this Regulation) provides that Member States shall consider ETS requirements to be satisfied and shall take no action against aircraft operators in respect of:
- **(a)** all emissions from flights **to and from aerodromes located in countries outside the EEA** in each calendar year from 1 January 2013 to 31 December 2016;
- **(b)** all emissions from flights between an aerodrome located in an **outermost region** of the Union and an aerodrome located in another region of the EEA for the same period;
- **(c)** the **surrender of allowances** corresponding to verified 2013 emissions from intra-EEA flights, with a deferred deadline of 30 April 2015 instead of 30 April 2014.[2]

## 🇪🇺 Council Directive (EU) 2015/652 — Greenhouse-Gas Intensity of Petrol & Diesel Fuels

### 📄 Key Identification

| **CELEX ID** | 32015L0652 |
| **Full Title** | Council Directive (EU) 2015/652 of 20 April 2015 laying down calculation methods and reporting requirements pursuant to Directive 98/70/EC relating to the quality of petrol and diesel fuels |
| **Document Type** | Directive |
| **Year** | 2015 |
| **Policy Domain** | Production, Technology and Research |

---

### 🔍 What the Directive Does

**Council Directive (EU) 2015/652** lays down **calculation methods and reporting requirements** pursuant to **Directive 98/70/EC** of the European Parliament and of the Council relating to the quality of petrol and diesel fuels.[1]

#### ⚗️ Calculation Method for Greenhouse-Gas Intensity

The method for calculating greenhouse gas emissions of fuels and other energy from non-biological sources should yield reporting of **sufficient accuracy**, so that the Commission can critically assess the performance of suppliers in meeting their obligations. The calculation method is designed to **incentivise suppliers to reduce the greenhouse gas intensity** of the fuel they supply, and is based on **average greenhouse gas intensities** that represent an industry average value typical for a particular fuel — reducing the administrative burden on suppliers and Member States.[1]

#### 🏭 Upstream Emission Reductions (UERs)

In order to incentivise further greenhouse gas emission reductions, **savings claimed from upstream emission reductions (UERs)**, including from flaring and venting, are included in the calculation of suppliers' life cycle greenhouse gas emissions. Only UER projects which start after **1 January 2011** (the date of the establishment of the fuel baseline standard) are eligible.[1]

#### 📊 Fuel Baseline Standard

The **fuel baseline standard** is based on the life cycle greenhouse gas emissions per unit of energy from fossil fuels in **2010**, using quantities of diesel, petrol, non-road gas oil, LPG and CNG consumed with data officially reported by the Member States to the **UNFCCC** in 2010. The fuel baseline standard **remains unchanged for the period up until 2020**, in order to provide regulatory certainty to suppliers.[2]

#### 📋 Reporting Obligations

For the purposes of Article 7a(2) of Directive 98/70/EC, **Member States shall ensure that suppliers use the calculation method** set out in Annex I to this Directive to determine the greenhouse gas intensity of the fuels they supply. Suppliers are required to **report data annually** using the template set out in Annex IV. For suppliers that are **SMEs**, Member States shall apply the **simplified method** set out in Annex I.[3]

#### 🗂️ Member State Reporting to the Commission

When submitting reports to the Commission, Member States shall provide data related to compliance with Article 7a of Directive 98/70/EC, and shall use the **ReportNet tools of the European Environment Agency** for submission. Data shall be transmitted electronically to the **Central Data Repository** managed by the European Environment Agency.[3]

#### ⚖️ Transposition Deadline

Member States were required to bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive by **21 April 2017** at the latest.[3]

## ⚖️ Legal Background

Under the Marrakesh Agreement establishing the World Trade Organisation (WTO), an **Understanding on Rules and Procedures Governing the Settlement of Disputes (DSU)** was reached, pursuant to which the **Dispute Settlement Body (DSB)** was established.

Whenever the DSB adopts a report concerning a Union measure taken pursuant to the anti-dumping or anti-subsidy regulations, the Commission may take **one or more of the following measures**:

- **(b)** Adopt any other special implementing measure deemed appropriate in the circumstances in order to bring the Union into conformity with the recommendations and rulings contained in the report.

rows.question:
Which 2013 Council Directive (Euratom) in the Science domain lays down requirements protecting the health of the general public regarding radioactive substances in water intended for human consumption, and which radionuclides does it bring into scope?

Metadata filters (apply with AND):
- Year: 2013
- Document type: Directive
- Policy domain: Science
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32013L0051 - Council Directive 2013/51/Euratom of 22 October 2013. It sets parametric values and monitoring for radioactive substances in drinking water, including radon, tritium and an indicative dose.
rows.actualResponse:
## Council Directive 2013/51/Euratom — Radioactive Substances in Drinking Water

The directive you are looking for is **Council Directive 2013/51/Euratom** (CELEX ID: **32013L0051**), adopted on **22 October 2013**.

---

### 📋 Overview

**COUNCIL DIRECTIVE 2013/51/EURATOM** of 22 October 2013 was adopted by the Council of the European Union, having regard to the Treaty establishing the European Atomic Energy Community, and in particular Articles 31 and 32 thereof.[1]**Article 1 — Subject matter:** This Directive lays down requirements for the **protection of the health of the general public** with regard to **radioactive substances in water intended for human consumption**. It lays down **parametric values**, as well as **frequencies and methods for monitoring** radioactive substances.[2]​[1]

---

### 🌊 What Counts as "Water Intended for Human Consumption"?

"Water intended for human consumption" means:
- **(a)** All water, either in its original state or after treatment, intended for **drinking, cooking, food preparation, or other domestic purposes**, regardless of its origin, whether supplied from a distribution network, a tanker, or in bottles or containers;
- **(b)** All water used in any **food-production undertaking** for the manufacture, processing, preservation, or marketing of products or substances intended for human consumption, unless the competent national authorities are satisfied that the quality of the water cannot affect the wholesomeness of the foodstuff in its finished form.[2]

---

### ☢️ Radionuclides Brought into Scope

The Directive explicitly names and regulates the following radionuclides:

#### 1. Radon & Long-Lived Radon Decay Products
Taking into consideration the **large geographical variability in the natural occurrence of radon**, the Commission had previously adopted Recommendation 2001/928/Euratom, which deals with the quality of water intended for human consumption regarding radon and long-lived radon decay products. **It is appropriate to include these radionuclides in the scope of this Directive.**[2]

#### 2. Tritium
It is advisable for those Member States required to **monitor water intended for human consumption for radon or tritium**, or to establish the Indicative Dose (ID), to carry out sampling and analysis **at least once per year**.[2]

#### 3. Potassium-40 & All Other Radionuclides (via the Indicative Dose)
The **'indicative dose' (ID)** means the committed effective dose for one year of ingestion resulting from **all the radionuclides whose presence has been detected** in a supply of water intended for human consumption, of **natural and artificial origin**, but **excluding tritium, potassium-40, radon, and short-lived radon decay products**.[2]

This means that **tritium, potassium-40, radon, and short-lived radon decay products** are monitored under their own dedicated **parametric values**, while all other detectable radionuclides (natural and artificial) are collectively captured by the **Indicative Dose** parametric value.

---

### 🔗 Relationship to Prior Legislation

The provisions of this Directive **supersede those of Directive 98/83/EC** as regards the requirements for the protection of the health of the general public with regard to radioactive substances in water intended for human consumption, since the Community is competent to adopt the basic safety standards for the protection of the health of workers and general public against the dangers arising from ionising radiations.[1]

---

## 📄 Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2115

**Full Title:** *COMMISSION DIRECTIVE (EU) 2015/2115 of 23 November 2015 amending, for the purpose of adopting specific limit values for chemicals used in toys, Appendix C to Annex II to Directive 2009/48/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council on the safety of toys, as regards formamide (Text with EEA relevance)*

- **Document Type:** Directive
- **Policy Domain:** Trade
- **Year:** 2015[1]

---

### 🔬 Background & Rationale

Directive 2009/48/EC empowers the Commission to adopt specific limit values for chemicals used in toys intended for children under 36 months and in other toys intended to be placed in the mouth. The adoption of such limit values takes the form of an inclusion in **Appendix C to Annex II** to Directive 2009/48/EC.[1]**Formamide** (CAS number 75-12-7) is used, among others, in the plastics and polymers industry, particularly as a solvent, plasticiser, or as a substance associated with a blowing agent used in the production of foam. In 2010, several Member States identified formamide in a range of foam toys, such as **puzzle mats**, which gave rise to concerns for the health of children through inhalation.[1]Formamide is classified under Regulation (EC) No 1272/2008 as **toxic to reproduction category 1B**. ### ⚗️ Scientific Recommendation

The subgroup 'Chemicals' recommended, at its meeting of **28 November 2013**, that emissions of formamide from foam toy materials be limited in Appendix C to Annex II to Directive 2009/48/EC to **20 μg/m³** after a maximum of 28 days from commencement of the emission testing. | **Formamide** | 75-12-7 | **20 μg/m³** (emission limit) after a maximum of 28 days from commencement of the emission testing of foam toy materials containing more than **200 mg/kg** (cut-off limit based on content) |[2]

### 📅 Transposition Deadline

Member States were required to adopt and publish the necessary laws, regulations and administrative provisions to comply with this Directive by **24 May 2017** at the latest, and to apply those provisions from that same date.[2]

### ⚠️ No Per-Tonne CO₂ Price is Established for Third-Country Flights

**Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 does not set a specific per-tonne CO₂ price** for flights to and from third countries. Instead, the regulation operates through a **temporary derogation (suspension)** mechanism under the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS):

- The regulation temporarily considers the requirements set out in Directive 2003/87/EC to be **satisfied** for the period until **31 December 2016** in respect of **flights to and from aerodromes in countries outside the European Economic Area (EEA)**.[1] This means operators on such routes were **not required** to surrender allowances for those emissions during this period — making a per-tonne price inapplicable for the derogated flights.

---

### How the EU ETS Works for In-Scope Flights

The EU ETS does not set a **fixed** per-tonne price. It is a **cap-and-trade** system where allowance prices are determined by the market. Under Directive 2003/87/EC, the methods for the allocation and issuance of allowances to aircraft operators are based on **verified tonne-kilometre data** in respect of the relevant periods — not a fixed price per tonne of CO₂.[2]

> **No per-tonne CO₂ price is stipulated** in Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 for third-country flights. ## EU Directive on Reducing Lightweight Plastic Carrier Bag Consumption

The directive you are referring to is **Directive (EU) 2015/720 of the European Parliament and of the Council** (CELEX: **32015L0720**), adopted on 29 April 2015.

### What Does It Do?

This Directive amends Directive 94/62/EC specifically **as regards reducing the consumption of lightweight plastic carrier bags**.[1]Lightweight plastic carrier bags are defined as plastic bags with a **wall thickness below 50 microns**, which represent the vast majority of the total number of plastic carrier bags consumed in the Union. Member States must include **either or both** of the following measures:

- **(a)** Ensuring that the annual consumption level does not exceed:
  - **90 lightweight plastic carrier bags per person** by **31 December 2019**, and
  - **40 lightweight plastic carrier bags per person** by **31 December 2025**
  (or equivalent targets set in weight).
- **(b)** Ensuring that by **31 December 2018**, lightweight plastic carrier bags are **not provided free of charge** at the point of sale, unless equally effective instruments are implemented.[2]

### How Are Member States Expected to Achieve This?

Measures to be taken by Member States may involve the use of **economic instruments** such as **pricing, taxes and levies**, which have proved particularly effective in reducing the consumption of plastic carrier bags, and **marketing restrictions** such as bans — provided that these restrictions are proportionate and non-discriminatory.[3]Those measures may vary depending on the environmental impact of lightweight plastic carrier bags when they are recovered or disposed of, their **recycling and composting properties**, their **durability**, or the **specific intended use** of those bags.[3]

### Exemptions

**Very lightweight plastic carrier bags** (wall thickness below 15 microns), which are required for hygiene purposes or provided as primary packaging for loose food when this helps to prevent food wastage, **may be excluded** from national consumption objectives.[2]

rows.question: Which EU directive updates the rules on the sweet substance produced by bees, in particular clarifying the status of the floral material the insects gather?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32014L0063 - Directive 2014/63/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 15 May 2014, amending Council Directive 2001/110/EC relating to honey (clarifying the status of pollen).
rows.actualResponse:
The directive you are looking for is **Directive 2014/63/EU**, which amends Council Directive 2001/110/EC on honey. Here is a detailed breakdown of what it covers:

---

## 🐝 Directive 2014/63/EU — Amending EU Honey Rules

### Background

Council Directive 2001/110/EC defines honey as the natural sweet substance produced by *Apis mellifera* bees. Honey consists essentially of different sugars, predominantly fructose and glucose, as well as other substances such as organic acids, enzymes, and solid particles derived from honey collection. Directive 2001/110/EC limits human intervention that could alter the composition of honey and thereby allows for the preservation of the natural character of honey. In particular, it prohibits the addition of any food ingredient to honey, including food additives, and any other addition other than honey. Similarly, that Directive prohibits the removal of any constituent particular to honey, **including pollen**, unless such removal is unavoidable in the removal of foreign matter.[1]

---

### 🌸 The Key Issue: The Status of Pollen

Pollen is part of the composition criteria for honey set out in Directive 2001/110/EC. Available evidence, including empiric and scientific data, confirms that bees are the origin of the presence of pollen in honey. Pollen grains fall into nectar which is collected by bees. In the hive, collected nectar containing pollen grains is transformed into honey by bees. According to the available data, additional pollen in honey can come from pollen on bees' hair, from pollen in the air inside the hive, and from pollen that was packed in cells by bees and released as a result of the accidental opening of those cells during the extraction of honey by food business operators. Pollen can therefore be said to enter the hive as a result of the activity of bees and is naturally present in honey regardless of whether or not food business operators extract that honey.[1]Taking into account the natural character of honey, and in particular the natural origin of the presence of constituents particular to honey, **pollen, being a natural constituent particular to honey, should not be considered to be an 'ingredient' of honey** within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011.[1]

This clarification is given legal effect in the directive's operative text:

In Article 2 of Directive 2001/110/EC, the following point is added: *"Pollen, being a natural constituent particular to honey, shall not be considered to be an ingredient, within the meaning of point (f) of Article 2(2) of Regulation (EU) No 1169/2011, of the products defined in Annex I to this Directive."*[2]

---

### 🧬 GMO Pollen — Important Caveat

This Directive is without prejudice to the application of Regulation (EC) No 1829/2003 to honey containing **genetically modified pollen**, since such honey constitutes food produced from genetically modified organisms within the meaning of that Regulation. Honey containing genetically modified pollen should therefore be regarded as being 'food (partially) produced from a GMO.'[3]

---

### 🏷️ Labelling Clarification (EC → EU)

Under Directive 2001/110/EC, if honey originates in more than one Member State or third country, the mandatory indication of the countries of origin may be replaced by one of the following: 'blend of EC honeys', 'blend of non-EC honeys', 'blend of EC and non-EC honeys'. Following the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, the European Union replaced and succeeded the European Community. It is therefore appropriate to clarify the relevant labelling requirements by **replacing the reference to 'EC' by a reference to 'EU'**.[3]

---

|---|---|---|
| **32014L0063** | Directive 2014/63/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council | Directly amends the EU honey directive (2001/110/EC), clarifying that pollen is a natural constituent of honey and not an "ingredient," and updating labelling rules. |

- **Entry into force:** This Regulation entered into force on the **twentieth day** following its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union, and is binding in its entirety and directly applicable in all Member States.[2]

rows.actualResponse:
The directive you are looking for is **Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087**. Here is a detailed summary:

---

## 📋 Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087
**CELEX ID:** 32015L2087
**Date:** 18 November 2015
**Policy Domain:** Transport
**Type:** Directive (short, ~2.9 pages)

---

### 🎯 What It Does

This is the **Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2087** of 18 November 2015, which amends **Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC** of the European Parliament and the Council on **port reception facilities for ship-generated waste and cargo residues**.[1]

---

### 🔑 Key Reasons for the Update

1. **New garbage categorisation:** On 15 July 2011, the International Maritime Organisation (IMO) adopted Resolution MEPC.201(62), amending Annex V to the MARPOL Convention on the prevention of pollution by garbage from ships, by introducing a new and more detailed categorization of garbage. The amended Annex V entered into force on 1 January 2013.[1]

2. **Consistency with IMO standards:** In the interest of consistency with IMO measures and in order to avoid uncertainty amongst port users and authorities, the table included in Annex II to Directive 2000/59/EC — which contains the different types and amounts of waste and cargo residues to be delivered or retained on board — should be adapted to the new categorisation of garbage.[1]

3. **Better waste delivery data:** In order to improve the regime established under Directive 2000/59/EC, which aims at reducing discharges of ship-generated waste and cargo residues into the sea, information on the types and quantities of ship-generated waste actually delivered to port reception facilities in the last port of delivery should be included in the table of Annex II.[1]

4. **More targeted ship inspections:** The exact data on the types and amounts of the ship-generated waste and cargo residues delivered by a ship in the last port is essential for an accurate calculation of sufficient dedicated storage capacity on that ship. Better targeted inspections will contribute to the effective operation of maritime traffic, by reducing turnaround time in ports.[1]

5. **Improved statistical analysis:** A systematic collection of accurate data on waste delivery would also allow a better statistical analysis of waste flow patterns in ports and facilitate the establishment of the information and monitoring system foreseen in Article 12(3) of Directive 2000/59/EC.[2]

---

### ⚖️ Key Obligations

- **Annex II** to Directive 2000/59/EC is **replaced** by the Annex to this Directive.[2]
- **Member States** shall adopt and publish, by **9 December 2016** at the latest, the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive.[2]

The directive you are referring to is **Commission Directive 2014/80/EU** (CELEX: **32014L0080**). Here are the key details:

---

## 📄 Commission Directive 2014/80/EU — Groundwater Protection

**COMMISSION DIRECTIVE 2014/80/EU** of 20 June 2014 amends **Annex II to Directive 2006/118/EC** of the European Parliament and of the Council on the **protection of groundwater against pollution and deterioration**.[1]

---

### 🔍 Why Was This Directive Adopted?

Based on the first review under Article 10 of Directive 2006/118/EC, not enough information was available to set new groundwater quality standards in Annex I for any pollutants, but **technical adaptations in Annex II** were found to be necessary.[1]**Common principles** for the determination of background levels needed to be applied in order to **improve the comparability of threshold values** across Member States.[1]

---

### 🧪 Updates to Pollutant Threshold Values

There is considerable potential for **nitrogen and phosphorus** in groundwater to present a **eutrophication risk** to associated surface waters and to directly dependent terrestrial ecosystems. Besides nitrates (already in Annex I) and ammonium (in Annex II), **nitrites** and **total phosphorus** (either as such or as phosphates) should also be considered by Member States when establishing threshold values.[1]A **watch list for pollutants** of groundwater was also established to increase the availability of monitoring data on substances posing a risk, including **emerging pollutants**, to facilitate the identification of substances for which groundwater quality standards or threshold values should be set.[1]

---

### 📋 Transparency & Reporting

The information provided by Member States on pollutants and threshold values proved **insufficient** in the first river basin management plans to enable proper understanding and comparison of results. The relevant requirements regarding the information to be provided were **clarified and complemented** to ensure the transparency of the chemical status assessment and to contribute to a potential **future harmonisation** of methodologies for establishing groundwater threshold values.[1]

---

### ✅ Implementation

Member States were required to **bring into force** the necessary laws, regulations and administrative provisions to comply with this Directive **within 24 months** from its entry into force, and the Directive entered into force on the **twentieth day following its publication** in the Official Journal of the European Union.[1]

---

rows.question: Which EU directive harmonises national laws on the milk-protein ingredients used in food for people, replacing an older 1983 measure?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015L2203 - Directive (EU) 2015/2203 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 November 2015 on caseins and caseinates intended for human consumption, repealing Council Directive 83/417/EEC.
rows.actualResponse:
The directive you are looking for is **Directive (EU) 2015/2203** (CELEX **32015L2203**). Here is a detailed breakdown:

---

## 📄 Directive (EU) 2015/2203 — Caseins & Caseinates for Human Consumption

**CELEX ID:** 32015L2203
**Full Title:** Directive (EU) 2015/2203 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 25 November 2015 on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to caseins and caseinates intended for human consumption and repealing Council Directive 83/417/EEC

---

### What It Does

This Directive concerns the **approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to caseins and caseinates intended for human consumption**, and it expressly **repeals Council Directive 83/417/EEC** — the older 1983 measure.[1]Council Directive 83/417/EEC provided for the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to certain **lactoproteins (caseins and caseinates) intended for human consumption**. Since its entry into force, several changes took place — in particular the development of a comprehensive legal framework in the area of food law and the adoption of an international standard for edible casein products by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission** — which needed to be taken into account.[1]For the sake of clarity, Directive 83/417/EEC was therefore **repealed and replaced** with this new Directive.[1]

---

### Scope & Key Definitions

**Article 1** establishes that this Directive applies to **caseins and caseinates which are intended for human consumption** and mixtures thereof.[2]

The Directive defines the covered milk-protein products as follows:
- **Edible acid casein** — a milk product obtained by separating, washing and drying the acid-precipitated coagulum of skimmed milk and/or of other products obtained from milk.[2]
- **Edible rennet casein** — a milk product obtained by separating, washing and drying the coagulum of skimmed milk and/or other milk products, where the coagulum is obtained through the reaction of rennet or other coagulating enzymes.[2]
- **Edible caseinate** — a milk product obtained by action of edible casein or edible casein curd coagulum with neutralizing agents, followed by drying.[2]

---

### Objectives & Harmonisation

The objectives of this Directive are to **facilitate, through approximation of the laws of the Member States, the free movement of caseins and caseinates** intended for human consumption while providing a **high level of protection of health**, and to bring existing provisions into line with general Union legislation on food and with international standards.[2]

---

### Transposition Deadline

Member States were required to bring into force the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive by **22 December 2016**.[3]

---

rows.question: Which 2015 EU directive sets a maximum permitted level for a foam-forming chemical in the materials used to make children's playthings?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32015L2115 - Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2115 of 23 November 2015, amending Appendix C to Annex II to Directive 2009/48/EC on the safety of toys to adopt a specific limit value for formamide.
rows.actualResponse:
## Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2115 — Formamide in Foam Toy Materials

The directive you are looking for is **Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2115** (CELEX: **32015L2115**), adopted on 23 November 2015.

---

### What Does It Cover?

Commission Directive (EU) 2015/2115 of 23 November 2015 amends, for the purpose of adopting specific limit values for chemicals used in toys, Appendix C to Annex II to Directive 2009/48/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council on the safety of toys, **as regards formamide**.[1]

---

### The Foam-Forming Chemical: Formamide

**Formamide** (CAS number 75-12-7) is used, among others, in the plastics and polymers industry, particularly as a solvent, plasticiser, or as a substance associated with a **blowing agent used in the production of foam**. In 2010, several Member States identified formamide in a range of foam toys, such as **puzzle mats**, which gave rise to concerns for the health of children through inhalation.[1]

---

### The Maximum Permitted Level

Article 1 of the Directive inserts the following entry into Appendix C to Annex II to Directive 2009/48/EC:

> **Formamide (CAS No 75-12-7):** **20 μg/m³** (emission limit) after a maximum of **28 days** from commencement of the emission testing of foam toy materials **containing more than 200 mg/kg** (cut-off limit based on content).[2]

This means:
- The **air emission** of formamide from foam toy materials must not exceed **20 μg/m³** after 28 days of testing.
- Emission testing is **not required** if the formamide content in the foam material is **200 mg/kg or less**.

---

### Scientific Basis

The subgroup 'Chemicals' further considered a nursery (room volume 30 m³) with a large puzzle mat (1.2 m², 720 g) and several other foam toys (thus adding up to 1 kg of foam toy materials exposed to the air). The air in that nursery (air exchange rate 0.5 h⁻¹) would contain **20 μg/m³ formamide** after 28 days if the formamide content in the foam toy materials were at approximately **200 mg/kg** and were completely emitted.[2]

---

### Implementation Deadline

Member States were required to adopt and publish the laws, regulations and administrative provisions necessary to comply with this Directive **by 24 May 2017** at the latest, and to apply those provisions from that date.[2]

---

rows.question: Which 2013 EU directive tightens the rules on selling portable power cells that contain cadmium and the small round cells that contain mercury?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32013L0056 - Directive 2013/56/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 November 2013, amending Directive 2006/66/EC on batteries and accumulators as regards portable batteries containing cadmium and button cells with mercury.
rows.actualResponse:
The directive you are looking for is **Directive 2013/56/EU**. Here is a detailed breakdown:

---

## 📋 Directive 2013/56/EU — Batteries, Cadmium & Mercury

**Full Title:** *Directive 2013/56/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 20 November 2013*

This directive amends **Directive 2006/66/EC** on batteries and accumulators and waste batteries and accumulators **as regards the placing on the market of:**
- **Portable batteries and accumulators containing cadmium** intended for use in **cordless power tools**, and
- **Button cells with low mercury content**

It also repeals **Commission Decision 2009/603/EC**.[1]

---

### 🔋 Cadmium Rules (Portable Power Cells)

Directive 2006/66/EC already prohibited the placing on the market of portable batteries and accumulators containing more than **0.002% cadmium by weight**, but portable batteries intended for use in **cordless power tools** were *exempted* from that ban.

The review concluded that, in order to gradually diminish the amount of cadmium released into the environment, **the prohibition should be extended** to portable batteries and accumulators intended for use in cordless power tools, because **suitable cadmium-free substitutes** — namely nickel-metal hydride and lithium-ion battery technologies — are available on the market.[1]The existing exemption for cordless power tools was allowed to **continue until 31 December 2016** to enable the recycling industry and consumers to adapt to substitute technologies across all regions of the Union.[1]​[2]

---

### 🔵 Mercury Rules (Button Cells)

Directive 2006/66/EC prohibited the placing on the market of batteries containing more than **0.0005% mercury by weight**, but **button cells with a mercury content of no more than 2% by weight** were exempted. Since the EU button cell market was already shifting towards **mercury-free button cells**, Directive 2013/56/EU moves to **prohibit the marketing of button cells with a mercury content exceeding 0.0005% by weight**.[1]The prohibition on button cells with more than 2% mercury by weight was allowed to continue to apply **only until 1 October 2015**, as a transitional measure.[2]

---

### ⚙️ Special Consideration: Hearing Aid Button Cells

As regards button cells for hearing aids, the Commission was required to **maintain under review** the exemption and report to the European Parliament and Council on the availability of compliant button cells **no later than 1 October 2014**, with a possible proposal for extending the exemption if compliant cells were not yet available.[2]

---

rows.question: Which 2014 EU regulation narrows the carbon-allowance trading scheme for airline flights so that it only covers journeys within the European Economic Area?
rows.expectedResponse: CELEX 32014R0421 - Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 April 2014, amending Directive 2003/87/EC and limiting the EU Emissions Trading System for aviation to flights within the EEA.
rows.actualResponse:
The regulation you are looking for is **Regulation (EU) No 421/2014** (CELEX: **32014R0421**). Here is a detailed breakdown:

---

## 📋 Regulation (EU) No 421/2014
**CELEX ID:** 32014R0421
**Full Title:** Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 April 2014 amending Directive 2003/87/EC

---

### 🎯 Purpose & Scope

This Regulation amends **Directive 2003/87/EC**, which establishes a scheme for greenhouse gas emission allowance trading within the Community, in view of the implementation by 2020 of an international agreement applying a single global market-based measure to international aviation emissions.[1]

### ✈️ Narrowing to the EEA

In order to sustain the momentum reached at the 38th Session of the ICAO Assembly in 2013 and facilitate progress at the upcoming 39th Session in 2016, the Regulation **temporarily considers the requirements set out in Directive 2003/87/EC to be satisfied for the period until 31 December 2016 in respect of flights to and from aerodromes in countries outside the European Economic Area (EEA)**. The Union emphasises that legal requirements can still be applied in respect of flights to and from aerodromes located in States of the EEA, in the same manner as legal requirements can be applied in respect of the emissions from flights between such aerodromes.[1]

### 📌 Key Derogation (Article 28a)

The Regulation inserts a new **Article 28a** into Directive 2003/87/EC, which:

- **Exempts from the ETS obligations** all emissions from:
  - **(a)** Flights **to and from aerodromes located in countries outside the EEA**, for each calendar year from 1 January 2013 to 31 December 2016.
  - **(b)** Flights between an aerodrome in an **outermost region** of the EU and an aerodrome in another region of the EEA, for the same period.
  - **(c)** Defers the surrender of allowances for verified **2013 emissions** from intra-EEA flights to **30 April 2015** (instead of 30 April 2014).[2]

### 🌍 Rationale

The aviation sector has a strong international character, and a global approach to addressing emissions from international aviation offers the best prospects for ensuring sustainability in the long term. The Union is endeavouring to secure a future international agreement to control greenhouse gas emissions from aviation while, in the meantime, limiting climate change impacts from aviation activities to and from aerodromes in the Union by autonomous action.[1]

---
